speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Come in, come in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number 6, Mr. Ignac Feldman.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Feldman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you got to the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, hold on. She-- can she come in? You go there, to my wife, maybe? No. Close the door.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? I don't know ?] you. She's your daughter. [INAUDIBLE]. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Out. My god. Mr. Feldman?

speaker: interviewee
text: They must close the door. Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you got to Holland, what did you do for a living there?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- when I come to Holland, I don't have the [INAUDIBLE] [? work. ?] And I start as a presser. You know, with iron. And I worked there, I was there four years to make a living, not to take help or anything. And so I was there four years. And-- to work and to support my wife. And my daughter was born in Amsterdam, supported two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your daughter's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rosa [INAUDIBLE]. And to-- worked till the immigration comes to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you come to Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Canada, we came in October, 1951. the 12th of October, 1951.

speaker: interviewer
text: You came by boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the trip over?

speaker: interviewee
text: The trip was no bad. Because it was a lot of people who was from the survivors. People who was in Russia. But I, my wife, we [? fall ?] [? in panic ?]. We hear it was the time of MacArthur. And I had Soviet, Russian, medals, papers. And I was really afraid. I don't like to be deported and to go back to Poland and something. And I have two children to bring up as free people, and to go to every country. I and my wife decided together, take everything from the war and to throw it to the Atlantic. Even the personally thankful [INAUDIBLE] of Stalin, with his handwriting. Because in any time, we know his dictatorship, his-- then he was-- he was so bad for humanity. He liked to create something for humanity. But he pushed back any social form thinking about liberty. From the history, like I mentioned in the beginning, we know the revolution in France take too much blood, not necessary. Not necessary, [INAUDIBLE], or [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE], [? Martin, ?] one guilting the other. So many people-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] was not necessary. The dictatorship from the Left, from Stalin, was the revolution from France till the revolution in Russia. Maybe it was 125 years. But he put back progress. He even [INAUDIBLE] from the [? humanities ?] then for 300, 400 years. He push it back with his terror, with his murderous action. And for that was no doubt to take everything-- even the medals-- and to throw it. Because we was not fighting for the glory from Stalin. I was fighting for humanity. I for liberty of religion, liberty from speech, and liberty of a mind. For that, I was fighting.

speaker: interviewer
text: My granddaughter, Galit asked me, zayde, how you know this, everything in-- about the history? And I don't know. This is in my mind. How did you find Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: I will tell you. Before I came to Canada, I have a little episode with a Canadian, with the council. This time, 1951, people who come to Canada from Holland is two kind. Workers-- they come alone. They-- till they find work. They find a room where to be. Or they have help. And then comes the family. Then comes people together with big capital. Because they come, right away, to the rich hotels.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came to the-- to the council and ask him, dear sir, I'd like to go with my family together. Ask me, how much money you have? I tell them this.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. You must go alone. When you've established, your family will come. I think this, if you have my papers, read. I was a war person. I escaped [INAUDIBLE] in 1939.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was fighting so hard. I leave my wife when the child was-- I don't see them over-- over two-and-a-half years. And now you tell me we supposed to be separated again till established? You think if I can survive this kind of condition I will not survive another [NON-ENGLISH]? You know, he go to this, these people will go together. This was the first encounter with a Canadian man. Neither money, no money, they go together. They cannot separate. This I will always remember. And there was a-- there was another joke. Now, when we-- can I talk a joke, also, about Canada? Certainly.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Then we go on after five years, they send-- today's three years or so. But at this time was five years a Canadian citizen. And we come to the [? court. ?] He ask-- he-- me what you [INAUDIBLE]. When I come to Canada, I was hired work to support my wife and two children. I send my children to the high school. Children.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a car and I make a living. And I'm very happy. I didn't have time to learn. Then he asked my wife, [? do you like ?] [? Canada? ?] Oh yes. He asked me, when you go out in the province, Quebec, in the east, and which province you come in? She told, [? too, ?] Ontario. I said please, sir, can I straighten this out? Yes, please. I told listen, if you come from the east, all of life was thinking to go to the west. So we cannot know the road back.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we go out on the province, come east, we come to New Brunswick. But you were [INAUDIBLE] and you didn't [? learn. ?] And you know it! I told her yes, because I know [INAUDIBLE] [? know history, ?] but forgive my wife. Then he start laughing. Then tells me, OK, one child is born in Russia. The other child is born in Amsterdam. And in Canada, I tell him, listen, sir, if I will have the Canadian citizenship, I will-- citizen [INAUDIBLE], I will go home full a Canadian. He start-- started laughing. When we come to take the papers, he come the first to us to-- to give us the papers. He was still laughing. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have grandchildren, Mr. Feldman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I have five.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Michael Feldman, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who has now, a little bit, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a split in the family. Because he married not a Jewish girl. I talk over with my son, not to be harsh to him. If my son and my daughter-in-law will be more humane to him, maybe this will be not the way. I talk with the girl. And I tell her-- this is the first grandson, after all-- he wear the name from my father, Moshe Yakov. And I tell her, listen. I can understand a [? name ?] for freedom. But, for freedom, don't bring in religion in your life, not you and not you be happy. But it comes to a wedding, I hear you like [? there ?] [? are ?] wedding. Christian wedding, no. If my grandson cannot have a wedding in the name-- in the [INAUDIBLE] of Moses, he cannot have a-- a wedding and a blessing from Jesus Christ. And take it in consideration. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead, Mr. Feldman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Take it in consideration, you cannot do it. And if you could do it, it is very bad. Because I'm not force her to take Judaism, to convert. But take a civil wedding and be blessed like that. But he took-- he took a Christian wedding. He is not-- he-- he not convert. And I talk with her, [? and tell, ?] listen. On my old age, I am ready to go, [INAUDIBLE] parents, to talk to your parents and to stop a wedding like that. Because in the long run, you will be hurt, both will be hurt. And up to date, he like to talk with me. He-- I'm talking with him. But there's a distance in the-- my son, in general, don't like to talk. Not my daughter-in-law, like to talk, because this is not [? with ?] their son. I tell him, Michael, I don't know [INAUDIBLE] the [? pull ?] is in your hand. A Jewish man don't go to the church wedding. If you go to the church wedding, you must know that your parents-- I can talk to you. But still, it is a distance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me the names of your other grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Now is Rachel-- Rachel. Very good girl. She married and was not-- not so good. Because he was from America. Even he is born here but America. She doesn't know it, and he was a running to other-- with other women. And even not two years. And she divorced him. Now she is in Japan. She have a high position, as a pedagogue, teaching, there in Japan. And she was here on the graduation from her sister, Miriam Feldman. Now, she was here in the same time when my wife was in the hospital. And you know, even on the graduation what you have here the picture, the graduation, was heartbreaking. Because months before the graduation, my wife was planning what kind of dress to wear, what kind of shoes, how happy we'll be about the graduation for our middle granddaughter, Miriam. But the graduation, my wife was in hospital, [INAUDIBLE] because of it, I couldn't go.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she [INAUDIBLE] me, the parents are in Israel. If you will not go, how did she will feel? Then I went there. But I couldn't talk, even, [? if it was ?] about the graduation.

speaker: interviewee
text: She is working. She graduate with honor, university. And she is working by [INAUDIBLE]. She have a good job. And I don't know what kind, maybe she would take one year rest. On the vacation, she went to Israel, to see their parents. Now, I have-- from my-- from my daughters-- I could take the [? age ?] from my daughter's side. Is Galit, a highly intelligent girl. She graduate [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] even from the seventh, the eighth grade, every teacher make the remark on the report, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was good to have you as a student. She have one B plus, and the rest, everything A and A plus.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE], she ask me-- she have a poem, what I will tell you if you have time. I will-- if you not have the time. She ask me a year and a half ago, she was not full-- eleven years old. She ask me [INAUDIBLE], Zayde, how long will it take to [? life ?] be a professional? It take maybe 10, 12 years. Oh, this will be good. [INAUDIBLE]. She turn around, she take in consideration my [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Zayde, it will be good if you will be around and to see that. I hope. I will try. You promise. I will try. Not up to me. It's up to God.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a man--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, we have, out of the youngest, is from my son, Ayela. And she is in Israel. She is only three weeks younger than Galit. So, we are all over the world now.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a man of history and a thinking man, if you had a message to leave your grandchildren from your experience, what would you tell them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before, not special to my grandchildren. My grandchildren took a lot from me. And they know what is right and what is wrong. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Security start at home. Teach them at home. I like the school, universities, not to forget [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what mankind can do. The law of jungle is staying on the [INAUDIBLE] [? down ?] the love of mankind. 1939, 1945. I don't see, when animals are not hungry then they not kill the other animals. But we, two legs animals, are killing one another for nothing. In our system, what unbelievable, unbelievable, like [INAUDIBLE] said, if you will take one lie, nobody will believe. If the lie will be [? higher, ?] then they lie will be the true. Take it in consideration. Take down the lie. Put the face from the mankind on this place [SOBBING] and see how the mankind act. 1939 and 1945. And then I'm finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Mr. Feldman.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is mine older sister, Nadja Feldman. A very beautiful person. Sensitive. She married Abe Laib Weizman. After couple of years, have a beautiful daughter, Anja Weizman. Nobody survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Her singing, in the [INAUDIBLE], her singing for the family, her life, [? nothing. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken, Mr. Feldman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Years before the war. I cannot remember--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --when this is. I'm here with my friend Sasha [INAUDIBLE] Liashchenko. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: You can see-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I am on the-- Where are you, Mr. Feldman?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am on the left side. Because I'm not a Soviet citizen, [BACKROUND NOISE] I don't have the star. And Liashchenko is in the right side and he weared the star. Three years, when [INAUDIBLE] was lying on the fire, and [INAUDIBLE] come to take out the dynamite, he protect me and I protect him.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture's taken 1940 [? or ?] 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife with my son Anatol in Russia. 1943 or '44 [? of. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture, I receive 1946, walking on the street, from a Polack, what he was working. I don't know even his name, but he know me. He tell me, this is taken out from a German who make the pictures. You as a Jewish man, keep them. And this is from concentration, how they put to the gas chambers after the-- into the crematorium after the gas chambers. This picture the same thing, is taken in Auschwitz after the liberation. This woman is still alive. It is half-dead and half-alive. This was the situation from the people, from the Jewish people, in the-- in Auschwitz [INAUDIBLE]. This is the picture. Yeah, this is the picture taken, I think, 1935 or '36. This is my wife's family of the grave from her grandfather, Szimszon Milsztajn. And this is her auntie and uncles. This picture's taken in the city of Amsterdam. If-- I think, 1949 or 1950. Walking in the street from Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This I am, Ignac Feldman, walking in the street from Amsterdam, not far from the synagogue where Baruch Spinoza was excommunicate from the Jewish community. There is not far this synagogue. This picture is taken on the boat, on the road from-- from Rotterdam to-- to Canada. I, Ignac Feldman, my wife, Lonia, my son, Anatol, and my little girl, Rosa Feldman. This picture is taken in Montreal. I, Ignac Feldman, my wife, Lonia, my son, Anatol, and [INAUDIBLE], my daughter. It is taken-- I not remember which year. This picture is from our golden wedding. From the left side is my daughter, [INAUDIBLE], Rosa. Then I'm staying. Next to me is Rachel, my grand-- my granddaughter. Then my son, Anatol. Then, Miriam, my granddaughter. Then Michael, my grandson. Then my-- my-- daughter-in-law, [? Lillian. ?] Then my wife. On the bottom is Ayela from the left, and [? Galiti ?] from the right. [INAUDIBLE]. This year is taken-- [? yes. ?] This year is-- this is taken 1987, in-- in-- in-- here, in-- in Toronto. This picture is taken of the graduation for my granddaughter, Miriam Feldman, from the left, and I, Ignac Feldman, in the right. Most important is, notice, my wife was this time in the hospital. This picture is taken-- I think-- I think, on the wedding from our granddaughter. My-- my wife, Lonia, and I-- the picture's taken on the wedding from my granddaughter, Rachel Feldman. This-- this is-- I'm Ignac Feldman, sitting with my wife, Lonia. And behind us is my daughter, [INAUDIBLE], and [? Chaim. ?]
